mnemonics.title: Four Conditions Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: MEMO: M-Mutual Exclusion, E-Hold and Wait, M-No Preemption, O-Circular Wait. Remember: 'MUTUAL HOLDS NO CIRCLE' or COFFMAN CONDITIONS (Coffman, 1971)

acronyms.expansion: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait - the four Coffman conditions
acronyms.usage: Used to remember the four necessary conditions for deadlock formation

acronyms.expansion: Resource Allocation Graph - visual representation of resource allocation to processes
acronyms.usage: Used in deadlock detection and avoidance analysis

acronyms.expansion: First Come First Serve - resource allocation policy that can contribute to deadlock
acronyms.usage: Used in conjunction with prevention techniques for resource scheduling

visualTips.visualization: Draw processes as circles and resources as squares. Show arrows from process to resource (request) and resource to process (assignment). Circular wait exists when arrows form a closed loop in the wait-for graph.
visualTips.purpose: Understanding how circular wait forms and can be prevented by ordering

visualTips.visualization: Show a process rectangle holding 2 resource squares while simultaneously waiting for 1 more resource square. Breaking this pattern prevents deadlock by either eliminating holding or waiting.
visualTips.purpose: Visualizing how to prevent this condition through resource allocation policies

visualTips.visualization: Assign numbers to resource types (e.g., Printer=1, Tape=2, Disk=3). Draw a staircase showing processes can only move 'up' the staircase (request higher-numbered resources).
visualTips.purpose: Understanding the increasing order constraint for preventing circular wait

analogies.analogy: Like requiring a student to get ALL books from the library before entering the exam hall, instead of borrowing one, studying, then borrowing another. This ensures no holding but wastes library resources.
Like traffic circles with one-way signs. Cars (processes) can only enter in one direction, preventing circular traffic jams (deadlock). The ordering prevents forming a closed loop.
Like a teacher taking away a student's incomplete assignment (resources) to give to another student who needs it urgently. The first student must start over (rollback).
Like making all books in a library shareable by making photocopies. The original (exclusive resource) is protected, but copies allow multiple users. Not always practical for all resources.
